Terms in this set (30)
A ligamentous connection, such as that between the bones of the lower leg, is called a ____________.


synostosis

syndesmosis

synchondrosis

gomphosis

symphysis
Syndesmosis
Movement of a limb towards the midline of the body is called ___________.


abduction

inversion

extension

flexion

adduction
adduction
A synovial joint is an example of a(n) ____________.


symphysis

diarthrosis

amphiarthrosis

syndesmosis

synarthrosis
diarthrosis
A twisting motion of the foot that turns the sole inward is called ____________.


plantar flexion

dorsiflexion

eversion

protraction

inversion
inversion
An immovable joint is a(n) _____________.


syndesmosis

diarthrosis

symphysis

amphiarthrosis

synarthrosis
synarthrosis
Bursae may be located in all of the following EXCEPT which areas?


around most synovial joints

within connective tissue exposed to friction or pressure

beneath the skin covering a bone

around blood vessels

tendon sheaths
around blood vessels
Decreasing the angle between bones is called ___________.


flexion

hyperextension

abduction

adduction

extension
Flexion
In an uniaxial articulation ____________.


only circumduction is possible

movement can occur in only two axes

movement can occur in all three axes

no movement is possible

movement can occur in only one axis
movement can occur in only one axis
Osteoarthritis is considered ____________.


an inflammation of tendon sheath

a chronic inflammatory disorder with an insidious onset

an inflammation of a bursa

a type of arthritis, referred to as degenerative joint disease or "wear-and-tear arthritis"
a type of arthritis, referred to as degenerative joint disease or "wear-and-tear arthritis"
The joint at the base of the thumb is an example of a(n) _______ joint.


gliding

ellipsoidal

saddle

hinge

pivot
saddle
The joint that permits the greatest range of mobility of any joint in the body is the ________ joint.


shoulder

wrist

hip

knee

elbow
shoulder
Which ligaments prevent backward displacement of the tibia or forward sliding of the femur?


tibial collateral

popliteal

fibular collateral

cruciate

patella
cruciate
Which of the following is NOT a function of synovial fluid?


lubrication

increases osmotic pressure within joint

shock absorption

protects articular cartilages
increases osmotic pressure within joint
The epiphyseal growth plate is an example of a(n) ___________.


amphiarthrosis

gomphosis

symphysis

synchondrosis
synchondrosis
Why are there NO blood vessels inside the joint cavity of synovial joints?


Movement would damage the delicate blood vessels.

Hyaluronic acid would react with blood.

Blood would prevent production of articular cartilage.

Blood would pool and limit the movement of the joint.
Movement would damage the delicate blood vessels.
Which of the following is NOT a synovial joint?


talocrural joint

acromioclavicular joint

costosternal joint

glenohumeral joint
costosternal joint
Which of the following is ONLY possible at the thumb?


adduction

opposition

flexion

extension
opposition
All of the following are structural classifications of joints, EXCEPT ___________.


cartilaginous

fibrous

synovial

osseous
osseous
Which of the following joints have a joint cavity?


the distal joint between the tibia and the fibula

sutures between the skull bones

gomphoses

the knee
the knee
"Weeping lubrication" is caused by the _______________ in a synovial joint.


bursae

menisci

presence of reinforcing ligaments

synovial fluid
synovial fluid
Which of the following factors would NOT provide stability to a synovial joint?


ligaments binding the bones together

well-fitting articular surfaces between the bones involved in the synovial joint

good muscle tone in those muscles that cross the joint

the presence of tendon sheaths and bursae
the presence of tendon sheaths and bursae
What percent of their length can ligaments be stretched and NOT snap?


100%

50%

10%

6%
6%
Which of the following movements is considered the simplest synovial joint movement?


rotation of the shoulder

flexion of the elbow

opposition of the fingers

gliding of ribs on the articular facet of a vertebra
gliding of ribs on the articular facet of a vertebra
Which of the following movements is an example of circumduction movement?


raising the arm laterally

pointing the toes

lateral bending of the trunk away from the body midline

a pitcher winding up to throw the ball
a pitcher winding up to throw the ball
Which of the following forces to the knee would be the most dangerous?


a lateral force to the extended knee

a twisting force to a hyperextended knee

a vertical blow

a force which tears the menisci free
a lateral force to the extended knee
The "superstabilizer" of the shoulder joint is the ___________.


glenohumeral ligaments

glenoid labrum

the tendon of the long head of the biceps brachii

the head of the humerus
the tendon of the long head of the biceps brachii
Which of the following factors contribute the most to the stability of the hip joint?


the ligamentum teres

the deep socket that encloses the femoral head

the muscles that cross the hip

the strong ligaments, such as the iliofemoral ligament, that reinforce the capsule of the hip joint
the deep socket that encloses the femoral head
Which of the following provides the most stability to the elbow?


The articular capsule of the elbow

The ulnar trochlear notch

The articulation of the radius with the condyles on the humerus

The anular ligament
The ulnar trochlear notch
"Water on the knee" is caused by __________.


fracture of the patella

torn menisci

dislocation of the knee

bursitis of the prepatellar bursa
bursitis of the prepatellar bursa
The release of the enzyme metalloproteinase is thought to contribute to the development of ___________.


gout

sprains

bursitis

osteoarthritis
osteoarthritis
Terms in this set (67)
Which of the following types of joints lacks a joint cavity and is held together by a fibrous connective tissue?
1. Fibrous joints
2. Cartilaginous joints
3. Synovial joints

a) 1 only
b) 2 only
c) 3 only
d) 1 and 2
e) All of these choices
A- 1 only
Which of the following types of joints do NOT have a synovial cavity?
1. Fibrous joints
2. Cartilaginous joints
3. Synovial joints

a) 1 only
b) 2 only
c) 3 only
d) 1 and 2
e) All of these choices
D- 1 & 2
Which functional class of joints contains joints that are freely movable?
1. Synarthrosis
2. Amphiarthrosis
3. Diarthrosis

a) 1 only
b) 2 only
c) 3 only
d) Both 2 and 3
e) All of these choices
C- 3 only
Which of the following is a type of fibrous joint composed of a thin layer of dense irregular fibrous connective tissue found between the bones of the skull?
1. Syndesmoses
2. Gomphosis
3. Suture

a) 1 only
b) 2 only
c) 3 only
d) Both 1 and 2
e) None of these choices
C- 3 only
A suture joint fits into which functional joint classification?

a) synarthrosis.
b) amphiarthrosis.
c) diarthrosis.
d) synovial.
e) cartilaginous.
A
The epiphyseal plate in a long bone is an example of which type of joint?

a) Gomphosis
b) Suture
c) Symphysis
d) Synovial
e) Synchondrosis
E- Synchondrosis
The joint between the first rib and the manubrium of the sternum is classified as

a) a synchondrosis.
b) a synarthrosis.
c) a cartilaginous joint.
d) All of these choices.
e) None of these choices.
D- All of these choices
Which of the following is NOT a common characteristic of synovial joints?

a) Contain a joint cavity
b) Are freely moveable
c) Articulating bones are covered with hyaline cartilage
d) Include elastic cartilage
e) Have ligaments holding the joint together
D- Include elastic cartilage
Which of the following is(are) made from dense regular connective tissue?

a) Ligaments
b) Articular cartilage
c) Articular fat pads
d) Synovial membrane
e) Synovial fluid
A- Ligaments
Which of the following structures include the fibular and tibial collateral ligaments of the knee joint?

a) Synovial membranes
b) Articular fat pads
c) Menisci
d) Extracapsular ligaments
e) Tendon sheath
D- Extracapsular ligaments
Another term for menisci is

a) articular fat pads.
b) articular discs.
c) articular spaces.
d) capsular fat pads.
e) capsular discs.
B- articular discs
Articular discs
1. maintain the stability of a joint
2. direct flow of the synovial fluid to areas of greatest friction
3. are made of hyaline cartilage
4. are only found in the vertebral column

a) 1 only
b) 2 only
c) 3 only
d) 4 only
e) 1 and 2
E- 1 & 2
Which of the following structures is used to reduce friction in joints?
1. Bursae
2. Synovial fluid
3. Accessory ligaments
4. Elastic fibers

a) 1 only
b) 2 only
c) 3 only
d) 4 only
e) 1 and 2
E- 1 & 2
Which type of motion results from relatively flat bone surfaces moving back and forth and from side to side with respect to one another?

a) Flexion
b) Extension
c) Gliding
d) Circumduction
e) Hyperextension
C- Gliding
Which of the following represents a type of movement where there is a decrease in the angle between articulating bones?

a) Flexion
b) Extension
c) Gliding
d) Circumduction
e) Rotation
A- Flexion
Bending the trunk forward at the intervertebral discs is an example of what type of angular movement?

a) Flexion
b) Extension
c) Lateral flexion
d) Hyperextension
e) None of these choices
A- Flexion
Flexion and extension usually occur along which plane?

a) Frontal
b) Transverse
c) Sagittal
d) Oblique
e) None of these choices
C- Sagittal
Moving the humerus laterally at the shoulder joint is an example of which type of movement?

a) Lateral flexion
b) Hyperextension
c) Adduction
d) Abduction
e) Gliding
D- Abduction
Which type of movement involves a continuous sequence of flexion, abduction, extension, and adduction resulting in a distal body part moving in a circle?

a) Gliding
b) Lateral flexion
c) Hyperextension
d) Circumduction
e) Elevation
D- Circumduction
Which of the following is not considered a "special movement" that occurs at only certain joints?

a) Depression
b) Protraction
c) Elevation
d) Supination
e) All of these are special movements
E- All of these are special movements
What type of special movement occurs in your clavicles at your acromioclavicular and sternoclavicular joints when you cross your arms in front of your body?

a) Protraction
b) Retraction
c) Inversion
d) Eversion
e) Supination
A- Protraction
Which special movement occurs when you bend your foot at the ankle in the direction of the foot's superior surface as would occur when you stand on your heels?

a) Inversion
b) Eversion
c) Dorsiflexion
d) Plantar flexion
e) Supination
C- Dorsiflexion
Which special movement involves moving your thumb across the palm to touch the tips of the fingers on the same hand?

a) Pronation
b) Supination
c) Eversion
d) Retraction
e) Opposition
E- Opposition
Which of the following is NOT a structural category of synovial joints?

a) Planar
b) Hinge
c) Condyloid
d) Suture
e) Saddle
D- Suture
In which type of synovial joint does a rounded or pointed surface on one bone articulate with a ring formed partly by another bone and partly by a ligament?

a) Pivot joint
b) Planar joint
c) Hinge joint
d) Ball-and-socket joint
e) Saddle joint
A- Pivot Joint
A condyloid joint

a) is also called an ellipsoidal joint.
b) is considered to be biaxial.
c) allows both flexion-extension and abduction-adduction of the joint.
d) can be found in the wrist.
e) is All of these choices
E- is all of these choices
Which of the following is NOT a factor affecting range of motion of a joint?

a) Structure of articulating bones
b) Tension of the muscles
c) Hormones
d) Disuse
e) Eversion
E- Eversion
B- Syndesmosis

a) Suture
b) Syndesmosis
c) Symphysis

Which of the following is a degenerative joint disease in which joint cartilage is gradually lost due to a combination of aging, obesity, wear and abrasion of the joints?

a) gouty arthritis
b) Lyme disease
c) bursitis
d) osteoarthritis
e) synovitis
D- osteoarthritis
Which of the following is an autoimmune disease that affects the joints?

a) gouty arthritis
b) Lyme disease
c) arthroplasty
d) osteoarthritis
e) rheumatoid arthritis
E- Rheumatoid arthritis
Which of the following diseases is characterized by sodium urate crystals being deposited in the soft tissues of joints?

a) gouty arthritis
b) Lyme disease
c) ankylosing spondylitis
d) osteoarthritis
e) rheumatoid arthritis
A- Gouty arthritis
Which of the following is a correct description of arthroplasty?

a) resurfacing of cartilage in a joint
b) reshaping of the ends of the bones in a joint
c) surgically replacing a joint with an artificial joint
d) injection of synovial fluid into the joint
e) a, b, and d
C- Surgically replacing a joint with an artificial joint
Based on the structural classification of joints, which of the following is a fibrous joint?

a) Synchondrosis
b) Synovial joint
c) Symphysis
d) Syndesmosis
e) Diarthrosis
D- Synedesmosis
A condition in which a muscle or muscle and tendon is stretched or partially torn is called

a) bursitis.
b) a sprain.
c) a strain.
d) a dislocation.
e) an arthrosis.
C- a strain
Which ligament limits hyperextension of the knee and prevents the anterior sliding of the tibia on the femur?

a) Fibular collateral ligament
b) Anterior cruciate ligament
c) Posterior cruciate ligament
d) Patellar ligament
e) Arcuate popliteal ligament
B- Anterior cruciate ligament
A joint in which there is a complete fusion of two separate bones into one bone is called a
a) synchondrosis.
b) syndesmosis.
c) symphysis.
d) diarthrosis.
e) synostosis.
E- Synostosis
Which of the following is NOT an anatomical component of the elbow?

a) articular capsule.
b) ulnar collateral ligament.
c) radial collateral ligament.
d) anular ligament of the radius.
e) tendon of the biceps brachii muscle.
E- tendon of the biceps brachii muscle
Bursae are saclike structures that are commonly found between bone and

a) skin.
b) muscle.
c) ligaments.
d) tendons.
e) all the other answer selections.
E- All the other answer selections
The hormone relaxin stimulates changes that lead to increased range of motion in the

a) shoulder joint.
b) pubic symphysis.
c) temporomandibular joint
d) knee joint.
e) coronal suture.
B- Pubic symphysis
Disuse of a limb, like would occur when the limb is in a cast, leads to muscular atrophy and _____ of the affected joint.

a) increased range of motion
b) decreased range of motion
c) increased fluid accumulation in bursa
d) loosening of the tendons
e) chronic inflammation
B- Decreased range of motion
Which of the following is NOT a factor that influences the type of movement and range of motion possible at a synovial joint?

a) structure of the articulating bones.
b) strength and tension of the joint ligaments.
c) use of the joint.
d) arrangement and tension of the muscles.
e) hormones.
C- Use of the joint
Which feature goes with the knee joint?

a) medial and lateral menisci
b) glenoid labrum
c) radial annular ligament
d) zona orbicularis
e) acetabular labrum
A- Medial & lateral menisci
D- Anterior cruciate
The line is pointing to the _____ ligament.

a) fibular collateral
b) posterior cruciate
c) oblique popliteal
d) anterior cruciate

d) posterior cruciate ligament

a) coracoacromial

C- Glenohumeral

a) coracoacromial

ضيف هي الاسئلة CHAPTER 11 هاظ اهم واحد
Terms in this set (64)
how skeletal muscles produce movement
- skeletal muscles produce movements by exerting force on tendons
- tendons attach to and pull on bones, and movement occurs
origin
attachment to the stationary bone
insertion
attachment to the moveable bone
belly
area of greatest mass, usually the center of the muscle midway between the origin and insertion
lever
- a rigid structure that can move around a fixed point called a fulcrum
- bones serve as levers and joints serve as fulcrums
- the lever is acted on by a load (resistance) and effort
lever example (the forearm lifting a weight)
effort = contraction of the biceps brachii
fulcrum = elbow joint
load = weight of object plus forearm
fascicle arrangement
- muscle fibers are arranged in parallel bundles within fascicles, but the arrangement of fasciculi in relation to the tendon can vary
- fascicular arrangement is correlated with:
1. the amount of power a muscle can produce
2. the range of motion a muscle can produce
parallel arrangement
muscle fascicles parallel to longitudinal axis of muscle; terminate at either end in flat tendons
fusiform arrangement
muscle fascicles are nearly parallel to the longitudinal axis of the muscle; terminate in flat tendons; the muscle tapers toward the tendons where diameter is less than at belly (bigger in the middle = more power)
circular arrangement
muscle fascicles in concentric circular arrangements form sphincter muscles that enclose an orifice (opening)
triangular arrangement
muscle fascicles spread over broad area converge at thick central tendon; gives muscle a triangular appearance
unipennate arrangement
muscle fascicles arranged on only one side of tendon
bipennate arrangement
muscle fascicles arranged on both sides of centrally positioned tendons
multipennate arrangement
muscle fascicles attach obliquely from many directions to several tendons
coordination within muscle groups
most muscle movements are coordinated by several skeletal muscles acting in groups rather than individually, and most skeletal muscles are arranged in opposing pairs at joints:
- agonist
- antagonist
- synergist
agonist
prime mover; like a protagonist, it is doing the thing/action
antagonist
opposing agonist force; like antagonist to protagonist in a story
synergist
muscle that aids a prime mover/agonist in a movement
naming muscles: direction
orientation of muscle fascicles relative to the body's midline
rectus
parallel to midline
transverse
perpendicular to midline
oblique
diagonal to midline
naming muscles: size

decreases joint angle
extensor

abductor
moves bone away from midline
adductor
move bone closer to midline
levator
raises or elevates body part
depressor
lowers or depresses body part
supinator
turns palm anteriorly
pronator
turns palm posteriorly
sphincter

tensor

rotator

naming muscles: number of origins

temporalis

naming muscles: origin and insertion

originating on sternum and clavicle and inserting on mastoid process of temporal bone
